name: Platform-Neutral Procurement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Introduce platform-neutral clauses into upcoming AltID procurement contracts → Systemic: Increase supplier diversity by 30% within 12 months, reducing single-vendor dependency cost by 15% → Strategic: Establish long-term resilience and reduce geopolitical risk in Denmark's digital identity ecosystem.
options: Adopt a phased procurement approach that leverages existing vendor contracts to introduce platform-neutral clauses.
Introduce a hybrid procurement model that integrates platform-neutral requirements with incremental vendor diversification.
Deploy a disruptive open-source identity wallet that replaces vendor lock-in with a community-driven fallback mechanism.
review: Controls Supplier Concentration vs. Procurement Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of regulatory approval timelines on rapid deployment.

name: Resilience-Focused Policy Framing
consequences: Immediate: Reframe digital identity risk narrative to emphasize continuity and resilience → Systemic: Increase stakeholder engagement by 25% and secure 20% more policy support funding within 6 months → Strategic: Embed resilience as a core value in national digital identity policy, reducing future crisis response costs.
options: Publish a risk assessment report that frames platform dependency as a national security threat.
Conduct a stakeholder workshop series that integrates resilience metrics into policy discussions.
Launch a public campaign that leverages decentralized identity concepts to shift policy priorities toward platform neutrality.
review: Controls Security Framing vs. Public Perception. Weakness: The options fail to consider the influence of commercial lobbying on policy adoption.

name: Technical Demonstrator Development Path
consequences: Immediate: Build two non-production demonstrators showcasing fallback authentication → Systemic: Validate fallback security at 99.9% success rate in simulated environments, reducing perceived risk by 40% → Strategic: Provide credible evidence that platform-neutral authentication is feasible, enabling policy and procurement leverage.
options: Develop a mobile-based demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS.
Create a browser-based fallback demonstrator that uses hardware tokens and open standards.
Construct a quantum-resistant, blockchain-backed identity wallet that operates independently of mobile OS integrity signals.
review: Controls Technical Feasibility vs. Development Cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider the scalability of the demonstrators for large user bases.

name: Coalition-Building and Advocacy Tactics
consequences: Immediate: Expand coalition to include civil society, academia, and security experts → Systemic: Increase advocacy reach by 50% and secure 3 new parliamentary allies within 9 months → Strategic: Strengthen political pressure for platform-neutral procurement and fallback mechanisms.
options: Formalize partnerships with existing privacy NGOs to co-author policy briefs.
Engage academic researchers to conduct independent security audits of current MitID dependencies.
Establish a cross-sector consortium that develops a shared open-source fallback platform and lobbies collectively.
review: Controls Stakeholder Diversity vs. Advocacy Momentum. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential conflict of interest among coalition members.

name: EU Standards Alignment Initiative
consequences: Immediate: Align Danish fallback strategy with EU Digital Identity Wallet framework → Systemic: Achieve alignment with at least 2 EU technical working groups, increasing EU policy influence by 30% → Strategic: Position Denmark as a leader in platform-neutral identity standards, attracting future EU funding and collaboration.
options: Submit technical contributions to existing EU working groups on identity interoperability.
Draft a white paper proposing platform-neutral fallback requirements for the EU Digital Identity Wallet.
Pilot a cross-border identity wallet that demonstrates EU-wide fallback capabilities using distributed ledger technology.
review: Controls EU Alignment vs. National Autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of EU regulatory lag on national implementation timelines.

name: Digital Identity Resilience Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Introduce a modular, micro‑service architecture for fallback authentication that decouples identity verification from platform integrity signals, allowing independent scaling and rapid patching of components. Systemic: Reduce single‑point failure risk by 70% and cut recovery time by 50% within the first year, as measured by incident response metrics, uptime statistics, and mean time to recovery (MTTR) benchmarks. Strategic: Build a resilient identity ecosystem that can withstand platform disruptions, ensuring continuity of essential public services and preserving national digital sovereignty, while also creating a scalable foundation for future identity innovations. The architecture will be governed by a cross‑agency steering committee that sets security standards, defines API contracts, and monitors compliance. It will leverage container orchestration to ensure high availability, and will integrate with existing national identity providers through secure, token‑based adapters. The cost of migration is projected to be 15% of the current platform‑dependent budget, but the long‑term savings from reduced vendor lock‑in and faster incident response are expected to offset this within 18 months.
options: Implement a conservative micro‑service fallback that uses existing FIDO2 endpoints and legacy identity provider adapters.
Adopt a moderate hybrid architecture that integrates hardware token fallback with cloud‑based identity services.
Deploy a radical quantum‑resistant, blockchain‑backed identity wallet that operates independently of mobile OS integrity signals.
review: Controls Architecture Complexity vs. Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the operational overhead of maintaining multiple micro‑services.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Amplification
consequences: Immediate: Expand engagement to include private sector vendors, end‑user advocacy groups, and academic researchers, creating a multi‑layered stakeholder ecosystem. Systemic: Increase stakeholder participation by 40% and secure 15% more public funding within 6 months, as measured by signed MOUs, joint research grants, and public‑sector budget allocations. Strategic: Build a broad coalition that pressures procurement toward platform neutrality, ensuring that future digital identity contracts embed resilience and supplier diversity clauses. The engagement framework will be governed by a stakeholder council that meets quarterly, sets shared objectives, and tracks progress through a transparent dashboard. It will facilitate joint risk assessments, co‑develop policy briefs, and coordinate public‑sector lobbying efforts. By aligning diverse interests around a common resilience narrative, the coalition will reduce the influence of incumbent vendors and create a unified front for policy change. The cost of coalition activities is estimated at 5% of the total project budget, but the expected return in terms of policy influence and procurement reform is projected to exceed 200% in terms of avoided vendor lock‑in costs.
options: Implement a conservative outreach program targeting local NGOs and civil‑society groups.
Adopt a moderate multi‑channel engagement strategy that includes webinars, workshops, and joint policy briefs.
Deploy a radical community‑driven open‑source platform that crowdsources policy proposals and technical contributions.
review: Controls Engagement Breadth vs. Decision Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential conflicts of interest among private sector participants.

name: Regulatory Incentive Design
consequences: Immediate: Introduce incentive mechanisms that reward vendors for adopting platform‑neutral solutions, thereby aligning market behavior with national resilience objectives. Systemic: Increase vendor compliance by 25% and reduce procurement costs by 10% within 12 months, as measured by the number of certified vendors and cost savings in procurement contracts. Strategic: Align market incentives with national resilience goals, creating a self‑reinforcing ecosystem where platform neutrality becomes the default competitive advantage. The incentive program will be administered by a dedicated regulatory unit that verifies compliance through third‑party audits, publishes a public registry of certified vendors, and offers tax credits or preferential procurement status. The program will be phased to allow vendors to transition gradually, minimizing disruption to existing services. The administrative cost is projected at 3% of the incentive budget, but the long‑term benefits include reduced vendor lock‑in, increased innovation, and a more resilient digital identity infrastructure.
options: Implement a conservative tax credit for vendors that meet platform‑neutral criteria.
Adopt a moderate subsidy program for small‑to‑medium enterprises adopting fallback authentication.
Deploy a radical blockchain‑based certification token that rewards vendors with verifiable compliance and unlocks preferential procurement status.
review: Controls Incentive Scope vs. Market Adoption. Weakness: The options fail to consider the administrative burden of tracking compliance.

name: Public Awareness Campaign
consequences: Immediate: Launch a national awareness campaign that frames platform dependency as a national security and economic risk, targeting both policymakers and the general public. Systemic: Raise public awareness by 35% and increase support for platform‑neutral policies by 20% within 6 months, as measured by survey data, media reach metrics, and public‑sector policy endorsements. Strategic: Build public pressure that forces policy change and procurement reform, ensuring that platform neutrality becomes a political priority. The campaign will be coordinated by a communications office that develops a multi‑channel strategy, including traditional media, social media, webinars, and interactive tools. It will feature real‑world case studies, expert interviews, and a user‑friendly risk calculator. The campaign will also partner with civil‑society organizations to amplify messaging and will track engagement through analytics dashboards. The cost of the campaign is estimated at 4% of the project budget, but the expected return in terms of policy influence and public support is projected to exceed 150% in terms of avoided vendor lock‑in costs.
options: Implement a conservative media brief that explains risks in simple terms.
Adopt a moderate social‑media campaign that uses infographics and expert interviews.
Deploy a radical immersive VR experience that simulates platform failure scenarios and demonstrates fallback solutions.
review: Controls Campaign Reach vs. Message Clarity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of high‑tech media production.

name: Cross‑Platform Interoperability Blueprint
consequences: Immediate: Develop interoperability standards that allow fallback authentication across all major mobile operating systems, ensuring that users can authenticate regardless of device vendor. Systemic: Achieve 90% compatibility across major mobile OSes within 18 months, reducing platform lock‑in by 60% as measured by cross‑platform authentication success rates and vendor adoption metrics. Strategic: Enable seamless user experience regardless of device vendor, strengthening resilience and fostering trust in the national digital identity ecosystem. The standards development will be led by a cross‑agency working group that collaborates with industry consortia, academia, and international standards bodies. It will produce open specifications, reference implementations, and certification criteria that can be adopted by vendors and public‑sector service providers. The working group will also conduct interoperability testing events and publish a public registry of compliant solutions. The cost of standardization activities is projected at 6% of the project budget, but the long‑term benefits include reduced vendor lock‑in, increased user choice, and a more resilient digital identity ecosystem.
options: Implement a conservative standard that extends existing FIDO2 protocols for cross‑platform compatibility.
Adopt a moderate cross‑platform API that integrates with both Apple and Google integrity services.
Deploy a radical decentralized identity protocol that uses distributed ledger technology to provide platform‑agnostic authentication.
review: Controls Standard Adoption vs. Technical Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the need for backward compatibility with legacy systems.

name: Digital Identity Resilience Governance
consequences: Immediate: Establish a governance framework that mandates platform‑neutral fallback requirements in all future digital identity procurement. Systemic: Increase governance oversight by 40% and reduce vendor lock‑in risk by 50% within 12 months, measured by audit scores and vendor diversity indices. Strategic: Create a resilient, adaptable identity ecosystem that can pivot to new technologies without compromising national security or continuity, thereby ensuring long‑term sovereignty and service availability.
options: Implement a conservative governance model that requires all new identity contracts to include a clause for platform‑neutral fallback authentication.
Adopt a moderate governance model that introduces a phased compliance checklist and periodic audits to enforce fallback requirements.

Deploy a radical governance model that establishes a decentralized identity consortium with blockchain‑based certification tokens, enabling real‑time compliance verification and automated fallback activation.
review: Controls Governance Complexity vs. Implementation Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the administrative burden of continuous compliance monitoring.

name: Innovation‑Driven Vendor Diversification
consequences: Immediate: Initiate a vendor diversification program that incentivizes small‑to‑medium enterprises to develop fallback authentication solutions. Systemic: Expand the vendor pool by 30% and cut procurement cost volatility by 20% over 18 months, measured by vendor count and price variance indices. Strategic: Build a competitive, resilient market that reduces reliance on Apple/Google and fosters local innovation, positioning Denmark as a hub for secure identity technology.
options: Implement a conservative vendor program that offers tax credits to SMEs developing platform‑neutral authentication modules.
Adopt a moderate vendor program that provides seed funding and joint‑development grants to promising startups.
Deploy a radical vendor program that creates a token‑based marketplace where vendors earn reputation tokens for delivering verified fallback solutions, enabling automated procurement decisions.
review: Controls Market Incentives vs. Regulatory Oversight. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of fragmented standards across vendors.

name: Adaptive Compliance Framework
consequences: Immediate: Deploy an adaptive compliance framework that dynamically updates certification criteria based on emerging threats and technology shifts. Systemic: Reduce compliance lag by 60% and improve audit accuracy by 35% within 12 months, measured by audit cycle times and error rates. Strategic: Ensure the national identity system remains compliant with evolving security standards while maintaining operational continuity, thereby safeguarding public trust and service reliability.
options: Implement a conservative compliance model that relies on periodic manual reviews and static certification documents.
Adopt a moderate compliance model that introduces automated compliance checks using static analysis tools and scheduled audits.
Deploy a radical compliance model that leverages AI‑driven continuous monitoring and blockchain‑based immutable audit trails to enforce real‑time compliance.
review: Controls Compliance Rigor vs. Operational Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the scalability of AI monitoring across all identity services.

name: Strategic Resilience Funding Mechanism
consequences: Immediate: Create a dedicated resilience fund that finances fallback authentication research and procurement. Systemic: Allocate 15% of the national digital identity budget to resilience initiatives, reducing long‑term operational costs by 25% over 5 years, measured by cost savings and resilience metrics. Strategic: Secure sustainable financial support for continuous improvement and rapid response to platform disruptions, ensuring the ecosystem can adapt without compromising service quality.
options: Implement a conservative funding model that earmarks a fixed annual budget for fallback research and procurement.
Adopt a moderate funding model that introduces a matching grant scheme with private sector partners to co‑invest in resilience projects.
Deploy a radical funding model that establishes a sovereign digital identity bond, backed by blockchain‑issued tokens, to raise capital for large‑scale resilience initiatives.
review: Controls Funding Allocation vs. Innovation Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of market volatility on bond issuance.

name: Future‑Proof Identity Ecosystem
consequences: Immediate: Design a future‑proof identity ecosystem that integrates emerging technologies such as zero‑knowledge proofs and decentralized identifiers. Systemic: Increase system adaptability by 70% and reduce integration time for new technologies by 50% within 24 months, measured by integration cycle times and technology adoption rates. Strategic: Position Denmark as a leader in next‑generation identity solutions, attracting international collaboration and investment, while ensuring privacy and resilience for all citizens.
options: Implement a conservative architecture that extends current FIDO2/WebAuthn protocols with optional zero‑knowledge proof extensions.
Adopt a moderate architecture that incorporates decentralized identifiers (DIDs) alongside existing identity services, enabling selective disclosure.
Deploy a radical architecture that replaces centralized identity servers with a fully distributed ledger‑based identity network, using zero‑knowledge proofs for privacy‑preserving authentication.
review: Controls Technological Complexity vs. Adoption Rate. Weakness: The options fail to consider the interoperability challenges with legacy public‑sector systems.
